intro.text:
The year is 1904. The Panama Canal is under construction. The United Fruit Company is consolidating its grip on Central America. And the Caribbean nations — many of them heavily indebted to European creditors — are on the verge of debt defaults that could invite European military intervention.

Theodore Roosevelt has a solution. In his December 6 annual message to Congress, he announces what will become known as the Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine:

"Chronic wrongdoing, or an impotence which results in a general loosening of the ties of civilized society, may in America... ultimately require intervention by some civilized nation, and in the Western Hemisphere the adherence of the United States to the Monroe Doctrine may force the United States, however reluctantly, in flagrant cases of such wrongdoing or impotence, to the exercise of an international police power."

You are a senior policy advisor navigating the implementation of this doctrine across three decades, four presidents, and dozens of interventions. The big stick is ready. The question is when — and how hard — to swing it.

newspaper.headline: PRESIDENT'S MESSAGE TO CONGRESS; THE MONROE DOCTRINE AND THE REVOLUTIONARY SPIRIT
newspaper.bodyText: President Roosevelt's annual message to Congress announces an expansive new interpretation of the Monroe Doctrine. The United States will now act as a hemispheric police power to prevent European intervention in unstable nations.

newspaper.headline: NICARAGUAN REBELS DEFY MARINES; SANDINO VOWS TO DRIVE OUT INVADERS
newspaper.bodyText: Augusto César Sandino, commanding rebel forces in the Nicaraguan highlands, has refused to disarm under the terms of the Espino Negro agreement. His forces continue to harass Marine patrols.

reaction_approve: Marines will be ready within 48 hours.
The region respects this kind of clarity.

A remark — reportedly made in Washington about Anastasio Somoza García — has been circulating the halls: "He may be a son of a bitch, but he's our son of a bitch." Whatever the source, the logic is the lesson of thirty years of Big Stick policy: stability on our terms.
